title: How PicoClaw Runs on Your Claude Code Subscription
query: Does picoclaw allow running with claude code subscription (i.e. running claude -p)?

chapters.label: The Big Picture
chapters.explanation:
**The short answer is yes** — PicoClaw fully supports running with a Claude Code subscription. It does this through a dedicated `ClaudeCliProvider` that shells out to the `claude` CLI binary in "pipe mode" (`claude -p`), meaning PicoClaw never touches API keys or authentication for this path. If you have Claude Code installed and authenticated (via a Pro or Max subscription), PicoClaw can use it as its LLM backend.

This story walks through how that integration works: from the provider interface that all LLM backends must satisfy, through the subprocess execution that calls `claude -p`, the JSON response parsing, and finally the configuration pathways that let you enable it. Key terms to know: **pipe mode** is Claude Code's non-interactive mode activated by the `-p` flag, and **model_list** is PicoClaw's configuration format where each LLM backend is declared with a protocol prefix like `claude-cli/`.

chapters.label: Provider Contract
chapters.explanation: The `LLMProvider` interface is the foundation every LLM backend builds on. It requires just two methods: `Chat()` for sending messages and receiving responses, and `GetDefaultModel()` for reporting the provider's default model identifier. This minimal contract — defined at lines 24–33 — is what allows PicoClaw to treat an HTTP API call to Anthropic, a local Ollama instance, and a `claude -p` subprocess all interchangeably. The design deliberately keeps the interface narrow: no streaming, no connection management, just request-response with tool support.

chapters.label: CLI Provider Struct
chapters.explanation: `ClaudeCliProvider` at line 13 has just two fields: `command` (the path to the CLI binary) and `workspace` (the working directory). The constructor on line 19 hardcodes `command` to `"claude"` — it assumes the Claude Code binary is on your PATH. There's no API key field, no token, no authentication configuration whatsoever. That's the key insight: all auth is delegated to the `claude` binary itself, which manages its own subscription credentials. This is what makes the Claude Code subscription path zero-configuration from PicoClaw's perspective.

chapters.label: Executing the CLI
chapters.explanation:
`Chat()` is where the subprocess invocation happens. Line 33 reveals the core arguments: `-p` activates pipe mode for non-interactive use, `--output-format json` requests structured output, `--dangerously-skip-permissions` disables Claude Code's built-in permission prompts (since PicoClaw handles tool execution itself), and `--no-chrome` prevents browser windows from opening — essential for headless and embedded deployments.

Lines 37–39 show a subtle detail: the `--model` flag is only passed when the model isn't empty *and* isn't `"claude-code"`. This means you can either let Claude Code use its own default model, or override it with a specific model like `claude-sonnet-4.6`. The prompt is fed via stdin (line 40's `"-"` argument and line 46's stdin pipe), and the response is captured from stdout. Context cancellation at line 42 via `exec.CommandContext` ensures the subprocess is killed if the request times out.

chapters.label: Message Conversion
chapters.explanation:
Before sending messages to the CLI, they need to be converted into a text format. `messagesToPrompt` (lines 68–90) handles this transformation. System messages are skipped here because they're routed separately via the `--system-prompt` flag. User and assistant messages get simple prefixes, while tool results are formatted with their call ID for traceability. Lines 85–87 apply a nice optimization: if there's only a single user message, the `"User: "` prefix is stripped, keeping the prompt clean.

`buildSystemPrompt` (lines 93–107) gathers all system messages and, if tools are defined, appends their descriptions. These are joined with double newlines and passed as a single `--system-prompt` argument, keeping the system context separate from the conversation flow.

chapters.label: Parsing Responses
chapters.explanation:
`parseClaudeCliResponse` on line 110 deserializes the JSON output into a `claudeCliJSONResponse` struct. After checking for errors (line 116), it extracts tool calls from the result text (line 120) and adjusts the finish reason accordingly — `"tool_calls"` if tools were invoked, `"stop"` otherwise.

Lines 130–136 handle usage statistics, combining input tokens with cache tokens (both creation and read) into a unified `PromptTokens` count. This is worth noting: Claude Code's caching system means repeated prompts can be much cheaper, and PicoClaw surfaces that information.

The response types defined at lines 172–193 mirror the real Claude CLI v2.x output format exactly. The `claudeCliJSONResponse` struct captures everything from session IDs to cost tracking (`TotalCostUSD`), duration metrics, and the nested `claudeCliUsageInfo` for token accounting. This fidelity means PicoClaw can report accurate cost and performance data even when running through the CLI.

chapters.label: Tool Call Extraction
chapters.explanation:
`extractToolCallsFromText` at line 11 solves a specific challenge: when the Claude CLI returns tool calls, they're embedded as JSON within the response text rather than in a separate structured field. The function searches for a `{"tool_calls"` marker (line 12), uses `findMatchingBrace` to locate the complete JSON object, then unmarshals the tool calls. Each call's arguments string is parsed into a `map[string]any` for structured access (line 42), while the raw JSON string is preserved in the `FunctionCall` struct (line 51).

The comment on lines 8–10 reveals this is shared between `ClaudeCliProvider` and `CodexCliProvider` (OpenAI's CLI equivalent) — a pragmatic reuse since both CLI tools embed tool calls the same way. Now that we've seen how the provider works internally, let's look at how users configure it.

chapters.label: Model Configuration
chapters.explanation:
`ModelConfig` at line 474 is the configuration struct that makes all this possible from the user's perspective. The two required fields are `ModelName` (a user-facing alias like `"claude-code"`) and `Model` (the protocol-prefixed identifier like `"claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4.6"`).

Notice how the struct's fields are organized into three groups: HTTP-based fields (APIBase, APIKey, Proxy) on lines 480–482, special provider fields (AuthMethod, ConnectMode, Workspace) on lines 485–487, and optional optimizations on lines 490–492. For the `claude-cli` protocol, only `Workspace` matters from the special fields — no API key, no auth method. The comment on line 472 explicitly lists `claude-cli` as a supported protocol.

chapters.label: Protocol Routing
chapters.explanation:
`ExtractProtocol` on line 45 is the dispatcher's first step — it splits a model string at the `/` delimiter. `"claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4.6"` becomes protocol `"claude-cli"` and modelID `"claude-sonnet-4.6"`. If there's no slash, it defaults to `"openai"` (line 49), reflecting PicoClaw's pragmatic choice of most-common-case default.

Down at line 142, the `claude-cli` and `claudecli` cases handle the actual provider instantiation. The workspace falls back to `"."` (current directory) if not configured. Then `NewClaudeCliProvider(workspace)` creates the provider — and that's it. No API key validation, no token exchange. The simplicity here is striking compared to the HTTP provider cases above it in the same switch statement, which all require API keys or auth configuration.

chapters.label: Legacy Config Path

snippets.content:
case "claude-cli", "claude-code", "claudecode":
			workspace := cfg.WorkspacePath()
			if workspace == "" {
				workspace = "."
			}
			sel.providerType = providerTypeClaudeCLI
			sel.workspace = workspace
			return sel, nil

chapters.explanation: For users who prefer the older configuration style with a `providers.provider` field, `resolveProviderSelection` in `factory.go` handles the mapping. Line 156 matches three aliases: `"claude-cli"`, `"claude-code"`, and `"claudecode"`. All three route to `providerTypeClaudeCLI`. This ensures backward compatibility — users who configured `provider: "claude-code"` before the `model_list` format was introduced don't need to change anything.

chapters.label: Factory Entrypoint
chapters.explanation:
`CreateProvider` at line 18 is the top-level function that orchestrates provider creation. It first merges any legacy provider configurations into the `model_list` (lines 25–36), ensuring backward compatibility. Then it looks up the model config (line 44), injects the global workspace if the model config doesn't specify one (lines 50–52), and delegates to `CreateProviderFromConfig` (line 55).

What's elegant here is that by the time `CreateProviderFromConfig` is called, it doesn't matter whether the user configured via the old `providers` section or the new `model_list` — the code path converges. For a claude-cli setup, this function retrieves the `ModelConfig` with `Model: "claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4.6"`, extracts the protocol, and creates a `ClaudeCliProvider`. The entire chain from config file to executable subprocess is remarkably short.

chapters.label: The Full Picture
chapters.explanation:
PicoClaw answers the Claude Code subscription question with a resounding yes, and the implementation is elegantly simple. The `ClaudeCliProvider` treats the `claude` binary as a black box — it feeds in prompts via stdin and reads structured JSON from stdout. No API keys, no OAuth flows, no token refresh logic. If `claude -p` works on your machine, PicoClaw can use it.

The architecture reveals a broader design philosophy: PicoClaw stays lightweight by delegating complexity outward. Rather than bundling authentication SDKs for every provider, it supports CLI-based providers that handle their own auth. This is particularly fitting for an agent designed to run on $10 hardware with <10MB RAM — every dependency avoided is memory saved.

To enable it, users add a `model_list` entry with protocol `"claude-cli/"` (or set `provider: "claude-code"` in legacy config). The factory chain routes through `ExtractProtocol`, matches the `claude-cli` case, and creates a `ClaudeCliProvider`. From there, every `Chat()` call spawns a `claude -p` subprocess, converts the JSON response, and extracts any tool calls. The result is a fully functional AI agent powered by your Claude Code subscription — no separate API key required.
